introSection: Rolling Thunder was a racing wooden roller coaster previously located at Six Flags Great Adventure in Jackson, New Jersey, USA. The ride was the park's first wooden coaster and debuted in 1979 during the park's fifth anniversary season.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
Rolling Thunder opened on June 6, 1979.

Trains on one of the ride's tracks were turned in reverse in 1984 and the ride was named Rednuht Gnillor (Rolling Thunder backwards).

During late 2012 and near the end of the 2013 season, it was rumored that Rolling Thunder would be removed.

In August 2013, Six Flags Great Adventure announced Zumanjaro: Drop of Doom. Rolling Thunder closed on September 8, 2013 in order for Zumanjaro: Drop of Doom's queue line to be built. It was demolished in November 2013.

The entrance sign and queue line were reused for El Diablo, a super loop, in 2015. El Diablo closed in 2018 and reopened at La Ronde as Chaos for the 2019 season.

The bottom of Rolling Thunder's first drop still exists and sits directly under El Toro.

sections.title: Incident
sections.content: A 20-year-old park employee fell to his death from the roller coaster on August 16, 1981. The employee was reportedly riding without the safety restraint in place.

fullText:
Rolling Thunder was a racing wooden roller coaster previously located at Six Flags Great Adventure in Jackson, New Jersey, USA. The ride was the park's first wooden coaster and debuted in 1979 during the park's fifth anniversary season.

Rolling Thunder opened on June 6, 1979.

Trains on one of the ride's tracks were turned in reverse in 1984 and the ride was named Rednuht Gnillor (Rolling Thunder backwards).

During late 2012 and near the end of the 2013 season, it was rumored that Rolling Thunder would be removed.

In August 2013, Six Flags Great Adventure announced Zumanjaro: Drop of Doom. Rolling Thunder closed on September 8, 2013 in order for Zumanjaro: Drop of Doom's queue line to be built. It was demolished in November 2013.

The entrance sign and queue line were reused for El Diablo, a super loop, in 2015. El Diablo closed in 2018 and reopened at La Ronde as Chaos for the 2019 season.

The bottom of Rolling Thunder's first drop still exists and sits directly under El Toro.

A 20-year-old park employee fell to his death from the roller coaster on August 16, 1981. The employee was reportedly riding without the safety restraint in place.



White track and supports.
